sections.heading: Why Coroutines for Networking?
sections.content: Android strictly prohibits network operations on the main thread — attempting one throws a NetworkOnMainThreadException and crashes the app. Before coroutines, developers used callbacks, RxJava, or AsyncTask to move network calls to background threads. Kotlin coroutines revolutionized this by allowing you to write asynchronous networking code that looks and reads like sequential synchronous code — no callbacks, no thread management, no complex chaining. Combined with Retrofit's suspend function support, coroutines make Android networking clean, readable, and safe.

sections.heading: Coroutines Networking Advantages
items: Sequential code style — network calls look synchronous but execute asynchronously without blocking the main thread
No callback hell — eliminates deeply nested callbacks that make code hard to read and maintain
Structured concurrency — coroutines are scoped to a lifecycle (viewModelScope); cancellation is automatic
Easy error handling — use try-catch blocks exactly as you would with synchronous code
Parallel requests — launch multiple network calls concurrently with async/await or combine with Flow operators
Automatic thread switching — withContext(Dispatchers.IO) cleanly moves work off the main thread
Integration with Retrofit — just mark API functions as suspend; Retrofit handles the thread switching internally
Cancellation propagation — if the ViewModel is cleared, all running network coroutines are cancelled automatically

sections.heading: Coroutines vs Callbacks vs RxJava for Networking

rows.callbacks: Error callbacks — must be handled in every callback
Manual — must track and cancel each callback
Low initially, painful as complexity grows
rows.rxjava: High — many operators and concepts to learn
Works but adds Java-style API on top of Kotlin

sections.heading: Coroutine Dispatchers for Networking
sections.content: Coroutine dispatchers control which thread or thread pool a coroutine runs on. For Android networking, the three most important dispatchers are Dispatchers.Main (the UI thread), Dispatchers.IO (optimized for I/O operations like network calls and disk reads), and Dispatchers.Default (optimized for CPU-intensive work like JSON parsing or sorting). Retrofit automatically moves network calls to its own thread pool when using suspend functions — you do not need to manually switch to Dispatchers.IO for Retrofit calls. However, Room database calls and manual file I/O do require explicit Dispatchers.IO.

rows.use_for: Updating UI, reading LiveData, navigating, showing dialogs
rows.notes: Default in viewModelScope — do not perform blocking work here

rows.thread: Shared pool of background threads (up to 64)
rows.use_for: Network calls (manual OkHttp), Room queries, file I/O, DataStore
rows.notes: Retrofit suspend functions switch threads automatically — IO is needed for manual calls

rows.use_for: JSON parsing large payloads, sorting, filtering large lists, image processing
rows.notes: Number of threads equals CPU core count — for compute-heavy work

rows.thread: Starts on caller thread; resumes on whatever thread resumes it
rows.use_for: Testing and special cases — rarely used in production code
rows.notes: Avoid in production — unpredictable thread behavior

sections.heading: viewModelScope — The Right Scope for Networking
items: viewModelScope is a CoroutineScope tied to the ViewModel lifecycle
All coroutines launched in viewModelScope are automatically cancelled when onCleared() is called
This means network calls are automatically cancelled when the user navigates away — no memory leaks
Launch a network call: viewModelScope.launch { val users = repository.getUsers(); _uiState.value = UiState.Success(users) }
viewModelScope runs on Dispatchers.Main by default — Retrofit handles thread switching internally for suspend calls
For Room or manual I/O in the ViewModel: viewModelScope.launch { withContext(Dispatchers.IO) { dao.insert(entity) } }
Never launch coroutines in GlobalScope from a ViewModel — they outlive the ViewModel and leak
Fragment and Activity have their own scopes: lifecycleScope — use for UI-only coroutines, not networking

rows.best_for: All networking, data loading, and business logic in the ViewModel
UI animations, one-shot UI operations, collecting StateFlow in the View
Collecting Flow/StateFlow from ViewModel safely in Fragment

rows.lifecycle: Never cancelled — lives for app lifetime
rows.best_for: Avoid — leaks memory; use viewModelScope or lifecycleScope instead

rows.lifecycle: Cancelled when the composable leaves composition
rows.best_for: Launching coroutines from Compose UI event handlers

sections.heading: Sequential Network Calls
sections.content: The most common networking pattern is a sequential call — fetch some data, process it, then update the UI. With coroutines, this is expressed as straightforward top-to-bottom code inside a launch or async block. Each suspend function call suspends the coroutine (freeing the thread) until the network response arrives, then resumes exactly where it left off. The result is clean, readable code that behaves asynchronously but reads as if it were synchronous.

sections.heading: Sequential Network Call Patterns
items: Basic sequential call: viewModelScope.launch { val user = api.getUser(id); val orders = api.getOrders(user.id); _uiState.value = UiState.Success(user, orders) }
Each await suspends until the previous call completes before the next one starts
Use this pattern when the second call depends on the result of the first call
Wrap in try-catch for error handling: try { val result = api.getData() } catch (e: IOException) { } catch (e: HttpException) { }
Set loading state before the call: _uiState.value = UiState.Loading
Set success or error state after: _uiState.value = UiState.Success(result) or UiState.Error(message)
For Repository pattern: the ViewModel launches the coroutine; the Repository contains the suspend function; the API call is in the suspend function
Suspend functions in the Repository execute on whichever dispatcher the calling coroutine is on — Retrofit handles its own threading

rows.code_description: val usersDeferred = async { api.getUsers() }; val productsDeferred = async { api.getProducts() }; val users = usersDeferred.await(); val products = productsDeferred.await()
rows.benefit: Both calls execute simultaneously — total time = max(call1, call2) instead of call1 + call2

rows.code_description: coroutineScope { val u = async { api.getUsers() }; val p = async { api.getProducts() }; combine(u.await(), p.await()) }
rows.benefit: coroutineScope ensures both complete or both are cancelled on error

rows.code_description: val results = awaitAll(async { api.getUser(1) }, async { api.getUser(2) }, async { api.getUser(3) })
rows.benefit: Fetches multiple resources of the same type in parallel — returns a List

rows.code_description: try { coroutineScope { val a = async { api.getA() }; val b = async { api.getB() }; Pair(a.await(), b.await()) } } catch (e: Exception) { handleError(e) }
rows.benefit: If either async fails, coroutineScope cancels the other and propagates the exception

sections.heading: Error Handling in Coroutine Networking
items: Use try-catch directly around suspend function calls — coroutines use standard Kotlin exception handling
catch (e: IOException) — network connectivity issue, DNS failure, or timeout
catch (e: HttpException) — server returned a non-2xx status code; e.code() gives the HTTP status
catch (e: Exception) — catch-all for unexpected errors; log but don't use as primary handler
Use runCatching { api.getUsers() } for a concise Result<T> wrapper: .onSuccess { }.onFailure { }
CoroutineExceptionHandler can be set on a scope for unhandled exceptions but does not replace try-catch
For Flow-based repositories, use the catch operator: flow.catch { e -> emit(ErrorState(e.message)) }
Always propagate errors to the UI as a UiState.Error — never silently swallow exceptions

rows.code_description: viewModelScope.launch { try { _state.value = UiState.Loading; val data = repo.getData(); _state.value = UiState.Success(data) } catch (e: IOException) { _state.value = UiState.Error("No internet") } catch (e: HttpException) { _state.value = UiState.Error("Server error: ${e.code()}") } }
rows.use_case: Standard pattern — handles all error types with specific messages

rows.code_description: suspend fun getData(): Result<Data> = runCatching { api.getData() }
rows.use_case: Clean boundary — Repository returns Result; ViewModel handles success/failure without try-catch

rows.code_description: suspend fun getData(): ApiResult<Data> { return try { ApiResult.Success(api.getData()) } catch (e: Exception) { ApiResult.Error(e) } }
rows.use_case: Custom Result type with domain-specific error handling
Reactive Flow pipeline — errors are caught and replaced with a fallback value

sections.heading: Timeout and Retry with Coroutines
sections.content: Mobile networks are unreliable — connections time out, servers are temporarily unavailable, and transient errors occur regularly. Coroutines provide built-in tools for handling these scenarios: withTimeout() cancels a coroutine if it takes too long, and retry logic is easy to implement with loops or the retry Flow operator. For network-specific timeouts, OkHttp's timeout settings are usually the first line of defense, with coroutine-level timeouts as an additional safety net.

sections.heading: Implementing Timeout and Retry
items: withTimeout(5000L) { api.getData() } — cancels the call if it takes more than 5 seconds; throws TimeoutCancellationException
withTimeoutOrNull(5000L) { api.getData() } — returns null on timeout instead of throwing
Basic retry loop: repeat(3) { attempt -> try { return api.getData() } catch (e: IOException) { if (attempt == 2) throw e } }
Exponential backoff: delay(1000L * 2.0.pow(attempt).toLong()) between retry attempts
Flow retry operator: flow.retry(3) { cause -> cause is IOException } — retries up to 3 times on IOException
Flow retryWhen operator: flow.retryWhen { cause, attempt -> cause is IOException && attempt < 3 } — conditional retry
Combine delay with retry: flow.retryWhen { cause, attempt -> if (cause is IOException && attempt < 3) { delay(1000L * attempt); true } else false }
Never retry on 4xx client errors — the request is wrong; only retry on 5xx or network failures

rows.description: Transforms each emission — convert API response to domain model
Catches exceptions in the Flow — emit a fallback value or error state
Retries the upstream on failure up to n times with optional condition
Waits before emitting — prevents rapid-fire search queries on every keystroke
Cancels previous inner Flow when a new value arrives — essential for search
Only emits when value actually changes — prevents redundant network calls
Emits a value before the first upstream emission — ideal for showing loading state
Performs a side effect on each emission without changing the value

sections.heading: Polling — Repeated Network Calls at Intervals
items: Use a while(true) loop with delay() for simple polling: while(isActive) { refreshData(); delay(30_000L) }
isActive checks if the coroutine is still active — exits the loop when cancelled
For Flow-based polling: flow { while(true) { emit(api.getData()); delay(30_000L) } }
Use conflate() on polling Flows to drop emissions if the collector is slow
Cancel polling by cancelling the coroutine or scope — no special cleanup needed
For WorkManager-based periodic polling (e.g., background sync every hour), use PeriodicWorkRequest instead
Respect device battery — avoid short polling intervals; use push notifications (FCM) where possible
Combine polling with exponential backoff on failures to avoid hammering a struggling server

rows.description: Emits a combined value whenever either source Flow emits
rows.example: combine(repo.getUsers(), repo.getProducts()) { users, products -> UiState(users, products) }.stateIn(...)

rows.description: Pairs emissions from two Flows one-to-one — waits for both to emit before combining
Merges multiple Flows into one — emits from whichever source emits first
Subscribes to all inner Flows concurrently — does not cancel previous inner Flow

sections.heading: Testing Coroutine Networking
items: Use runTest { } from kotlinx-coroutines-test for testing suspend functions and Flow collection
MockWebServer (from OkHttp) provides a real local HTTP server — enqueue mock responses for testing
enqueueResponse: server.enqueue(MockResponse().setBody(json).setResponseCode(200))
Provide the MockWebServer's base URL to Retrofit: server.url("/").toString()
Use TestCoroutineDispatcher or UnconfinedTestDispatcher for immediate coroutine execution in tests
Test error scenarios by enqueuing error responses: server.enqueue(MockResponse().setResponseCode(500))
Use Turbine library for Flow testing: flow.test { assertEquals(expected, awaitItem()) }
Inject fake Repository implementations in ViewModel tests to isolate from real network calls

rows.description: System service for checking current network status and capabilities
Callback-based listener for network connectivity changes
Wrap NetworkCallback in a callbackFlow for reactive connectivity monitoring
Combine connectivity Flow with data Flow — retry network call when connection is restored

sections.heading: Best Practices for Coroutine Networking
items: Always launch network coroutines from viewModelScope — never GlobalScope
Put network calls in the Repository as suspend functions — ViewModel only orchestrates, Repository executes
Use withContext(Dispatchers.IO) only for non-Retrofit I/O — Retrofit handles thread switching internally
Always handle both IOException (network error) and HttpException (server error) separately
Never perform network calls in onCreate(), onStart(), or onResume() directly — use ViewModel
Use StateFlow with WhileSubscribed(5000) for network-driven state — prevents unnecessary re-fetching on rotation
Implement loading, success, and error states as a sealed UiState class — expose all three to the View
Use coroutineScope { } for parallel calls — it ensures all child coroutines complete or fail together

sections.heading: Common Coroutine Networking Mistakes to Avoid

rows.mistake: Making network calls on the main thread
rows.fix: Retrofit suspend functions run on OkHttp's background thread pool automatically; Room needs explicit Dispatchers.IO
Use viewModelScope — it ties the coroutine lifetime to the ViewModel and cancels automatically
Catch IOException and HttpException separately — they have different causes and require different recovery strategies

rows.mistake: Not cancelling coroutines when ViewModel is cleared
rows.fix: viewModelScope handles this automatically — avoid launching coroutines outside viewModelScope in the ViewModel

rows.mistake: Running async calls sequentially instead of in parallel
rows.fix: Use async { } and await() for independent calls that can run simultaneously to reduce total wait time

rows.mistake: Collecting Flow in lifecycleScope without repeatOnLifecycle
rows.fix: Use repeatOnLifecycle(STARTED) to stop collecting when Fragment is in background — prevents wasted updates

rows.mistake: Using delay() to wait for a network result
rows.fix: Use await() on Deferred or collect() on Flow — never guess timing with delay()

sections.heading: Summary
sections.content: Kotlin coroutines transform Android networking from a complex, callback-driven challenge into clean, sequential, readable code. By combining Retrofit's suspend function support with viewModelScope for lifecycle management, structured error handling with try-catch, and Flow operators for reactive pipelines, you get a networking architecture that is safe, cancellable, testable, and easy to reason about. Master the patterns of sequential calls, parallel async/await, Flow-based reactive networking, and retry strategies, and you will handle virtually every real-world networking scenario in Android apps with confidence.
